**DESIGN BRIEF**

*Project MiniClaw — Trade Show Promotional Gripper*

| To: | Engineering Team (you) |
| :---- | :---- |
| **From:** | Jordan Chen, Engineering Manager, Gripper Systems Division |
| **Date:** | March 31, 2026 |
| **Priority:** | High — Prototype needed for RobotExpo 2026 (June 10–12) |

# **Background**

Our competitors at Hiwonder sell the **BigClaw**, a compact servo-driven mechanical gripper used in hobby robotics and education. It’s well-designed and popular. Here’s what we’re up against:

![][image1]

*Hiwonder BigClaw gripping colored blocks (source: hiwonder.com)*

![][image2]

*BigClaw servo specs: LDX-335MG, 20 kg·cm stall torque at 7.4V (source: hiwonder.com)*

![][image3]

*BigClaw dimensional drawing: 115 × 57 × 69 mm, 162g with servo (source: hiwonder.com)*

Full product details: 

[https://www.hiwonder.com/products/bigclaw-mechanical-gripper](https://www.hiwonder.com/products/bigclaw-mechanical-gripper?variant=32473859620951)

## **BigClaw Reference Specifications**

| Parameter | BigClaw Value |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Overall size** | 115 × 57 × 69 mm |
| **Weight (with servo)** | 162 g |
| **Material** | Hard aluminum alloy |
| **Transfer method** | Gear \+ connecting rod (linkage) |
| **Clamping range** | 0–86 mm |
| **Maximum clamping force** | 500 g (\~4.9 N) |
| **Servo** | LDX-335MG, 20 kg·cm stall torque at 7.4V |

# **The Ask**

Marketing wants a mechanical gripper to give away at RobotExpo 2026 in June. Something people will keep on their desks — a fidget toy that also shows off our engineering. The concept: a scaled-down, 3D-printable version of the BigClaw that visitors can turn by hand to open and close. No electronics, no batteries, just satisfying mechanical action.

Your job is to design this gripper. Here are the requirements:

## **Design Requirements**

1. **Scale down \~20% from the BigClaw.** Target envelope: approximately 92 × 46 × 55 mm. We’re working with smaller components on the assembly line; the full-size BigClaw is slightly too bulky for a pocket giveaway.

2. **Entirely 3D printable (except the input wheel).** Everything — housing, gears, linkages, jaw arms, pins — must be printable in PLA or PETG on a standard FDM printer. No metal fasteners, no bearings, no purchased parts. Think about additive manufacturing constraints: minimum wall thickness, pin diameter, print orientation, clearances between moving parts.

3. **Hand-driven input.** Instead of a servo, the input is a thumb wheel or knob that the user turns to open and close the gripper. It should take only 2–3 full turns to go from fully open to fully closed. The gear reduction should amplify hand torque so the mechanism feels satisfying and smooth, not forced.

4. **Meaningful grip force.** When fully closed on a small object (\~25 mm), the gripper should apply at least 5–8 N of gripping force per jaw with moderate hand effort. The mechanism should be designed so that a servo motor could replace the thumb wheel in a future motorized version — design for a 0.3 N·m servo stall torque as your baseline.

5. **Easy to print and assemble.** This will be produced in quantity (500+ units) by our fab team, not one-off prototypes. Design for printability: minimize supports, use snap-fit or press-fit assembly where possible, and keep total part count under 15 pieces. Assembly should take under 10 minutes by someone following a simple instruction card.

6. **Trade show appeal.** It should look and feel like precision engineering, not a toy. Smooth action, clean lines, ACME branding area on the housing. People should want to pick it up and play with it. Consider including our tagline area: “ACME Robotics — Engineered to Grip.”

## **Key Differences from the BigClaw**

|  | BigClaw (reference) | MiniClaw (your design) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Size** | 115 × 57 × 69 mm | \~92 × 46 × 55 mm |
| **Material** | Machined aluminum | 3D printed PLA/PETG |
| **Drive** | Servo motor (LDX-335MG) | Thumb wheel (manual), servo-compatible |
| **Fasteners** | Metal screws and pins | Printed pins, snap-fits, press-fits |
| **Purpose** | Functional robotic end-effector | Desk toy / trade show giveaway |
| **Quantity** | Individual purchase | 500+ units for RobotExpo 2026 |

# **What I Need from You**

I need the mechanical design of the gear train and linkage system. Specifically:

7. A requirements document capturing the engineering constraints and success criteria

8. Gear train sizing: module, tooth counts, gear diameters, face width, and tooth stress verification for 3D-printed PLA gears

9. Linkage geometry showing how the gear output connects to the jaw arms (study the BigClaw’s gear \+ connecting rod approach)

10. A calculation tool (Python script or notebook) that takes the key parameters and outputs the design values

11. An honest assessment of where your design needs further work before we send it to prototyping

You are expected to use AI tools (Copilot, Claude, ChatGPT, etc.) to help with this work. In fact, I want to see how you direct AI on an engineering problem — your process for managing the collaboration is as important as the final numbers. Document your iteration: what you asked, what you got, what you changed.

| Timeline I need the initial design package by end of day Monday, April 13\. We’ll review together the following week and decide whether to move to CAD and prototyping. Don’t wait for perfection — I’d rather see an honest first pass with identified gaps than a polished document that hides uncertainties. |
| :---- |

*— Jordan*

Jordan Chen | Engineering Manager, Gripper Systems Division | ACME Robotics Inc.
